---
#title: "Chapter 4: Non-linear mixed effects models"
#author: "Dagan Lonsdale"
#bibliography: chapter4.bib
#csl: aa.csl

#output:
#  bookdown::html_document2:
#    fig_caption : TRUE
#    number_sections: no
#    toc: yes
#    toc_float: yes
#    latex_engine: xelatex
#    highlight: tango
#    extra_dependencies: "subfig"
#header-includes:
#  - \usepackage{float}
#  - \floatplacement{table}{H}
#  - \usepackage{booktabs}
#  - \usepackage{caption}
#  - \captionsetup{labelfont=bf,margin=12pt}
#urlcolor: blue

title: "Non-linear mixed effects models"
output:
  bookdown::html_document2:
    fig_caption : TRUE
    number_sections: no
    toc: yes
    toc_float: yes

header-includes:
  - \usepackage{colortbl}
  - \usepackage{multirow}
  - \usepackage{float}
  - \floatplacement{figure}{H}
  - \floatplacement{table}{H}
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \captionsetup{labelfont=bf,margin=12pt} 
  - \newcommand{\blandscape}{\begin{landscape}}
  - \newcommand{\elandscape}{\end{landscape}}

---

<style>
h1.title {
  font-size: 48px;

  text-align: center;
}
h1 {
  font-size: 24px;
}
h2 {
  font-size: 20px;
}
h3 {
  font-size: 16px;
}

h4.author { 
  text-align: center;
}
body {
text-align: justify}
</style>

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, fig.pos= "H")
library(tidyverse)
library(kableExtra)
library(gridExtra)
library(PKNCA) 
library(RColorBrewer)
library(broom)
library(nlmixr2)
library(nlmixr2data)
```

# Learning outcomes  

* List advantages and disadvantages of compartmental analysis
* Describe the difference between a fixed effect and a random effect in a population pharmacokinetic model
* Interpret the key parameter estimates from a fitted model (clearance and volume of distribution)

# Tasks

* Fit a one-compartment IV bolus model for the 'Healthy' dataset
* Fit a one-compartment IV bolus model for the 'Sepsis' dataset
* Compare volume of distribution and clearance values for the two datasets
* Compare volume of distribution and clearance values using non-compartmental and compartmental analysis

### Extension tasks

* Fit two-compartment models to the datasets
* Create diagnostic plots for your models (See Additional Materials)

## Datasets for tasks

## Dataset used in workbook example



# Introduction 

'Rich' data are where there are lots of samples for each individual at each time point. This kind of data is really easy to produce in early-phase trials, including entry-into-human studies. 

However, at later phases of drug development, including post-marketing, pharmacokinetic data are often more challenging to obtain. In addition, rigid sampling times are less easy to implement outside of the confines of a dedicated pharmacokinetic study as patients or study participants are often at clinic or study visits at irregular times and so sampling may be somewhat opportunistic. Self-administered doses are also less likely to be accurately taken at prescribed times. These factors make modelling data using the non-compartmental methods we have used so far challenging and less accurate.

The purpose of this session, is to introduce the concept of non-linear mixed-effects modelling. This will include introduction to the modelling package <TT>nlmixr</TT>. This package is challenging to install on your own machine, <TT>install.packages('nlmixr2')</TT> may work. If you run a Windows operating system, you are likely to also need <TT>rtools</TT>. You will need to follow instructions online on how to install and download this. Installation on an Apple Mac usually requires <TT>Xcode command line tools</TT> to be downloaded. We recommend using [Posit Cloud](https://posit.cloud/) for this session. 

## Advantages and disadvantages of non-linear mixed-effects models

**Advantages of non-linear mixed-effects approach**

* Enables modelling of sparse data, useful in later phase studies
* Allows more complex identification of covariate effects (for example organ function)
* Alternative sampling and modelling may allow for populations to be studied that are not straightforward to study in traditional phase-0/1 design (for example children, or drugs in pregnancy)
* Allows complex simulations which can inform subsequent study design and drug dosing

**Disadvantages of non-linear mixed-effects approach**

* Feels inherently more complex which can make people anxious about *undertaking* or *understanding* the process
* Computationally expensive
* Time consuming
* Often requires time cleaning/organising data from multiple sources 

# What is the point of a model?

It is clear that understanding data from a clinical trial is important. Pharmacokinetic data is the same as any other output from a trial. It is important to understand the data, what it means for the absorption, distribution, metabolism and excretion (ADME) of the compound AND it allows us to make suggestions for the next stage of drug development. This might include dose selection, frequency of administration or even decisions on formulation.

Compartmental pharmacokinetic models simplify complex human physiology by describing the concentration-time course of a drug with (relatively) simple mathematical equations. These mathematical equations help to quantify ADME processes through the estimation of values for parameters such as clearance and volume of distribution.

## One-compartment IV bolus model

In the one compartment model IV bolus model, the body is simplified and represented as a single box, into which the dose of the drug is deposited instantaneously. Drug is assumed to distribute evenly and instantaneously throughout this compartment such that the contents of the box is assumed to be the same wherever you are within it.


```{r onecpt, echo = FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='One-compartment model',fig.height=2,out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("onecpt1.png")
```


This single compartment is assumed to receive the drug instantly at the time of the intravenous dose. The compartment has a volume $V_1$, which in a one-compartment model is equal to the volume of distribution ($V_d$) of the drug. The concentration in this compartment is equal to the plasma concentration ($C_p$) and the amount of drug in the compartment ($A_1$) is equal to the total amount of drug in the body ($A_b$). Elimination of the drug is usually assumed to be a first-order process with elimination rate constant $k_e$. 

## Relationship between $k_e$, $V_d$ and $Cl$ in the IV bolus model

$k_e, V_1$ and clearance ($Cl$) are related \begin{equation} k_e=\frac{Cl}{V_1} \end{equation}.  

## Concentration-time equation for the one-compartment IV bolus model

The concentration $C$ at time $t$ for the one-compartment IV bolus model is \begin{equation} C=C_0e^{-kt} \end{equation} or, if you prefer to use clearance and volume of distribution \begin{equation} C=C_0e^{-\frac{Cl}{Vd}t} \end{equation} $C_0=$ Drug concentration at time $t=0$. 

Taking the log of this equation provides us with $$log(C)=log(C_0)-k_et$$ which is an equation of a straight line, i.e. of the form $$y=mx+c$$ Data from a pharmacokinetic experiment may therefore be analysed using a compartmental approach and linear regression analysis, as calculating the gradient provides $k_e$ and the intercept provides $C_0$ from which $V_d$ may be calculated. 

Real world experiments do not generate 'clean' data, that produce nice smooth lines of best fit that pass through all the data points. There is additional (residual) variability. Let's look at an example from a single individual with a realistic time-concentration profile (Figure \@ref(fig:single)). 

```{r single,echo=F,eval=T,fig.cap="PK data from an individual", fig.subcap=c('Standard plot','Semi-log plot with linear regression'),fig.ncol = 2, out.width = "50%", fig.align = 'center', warning=F,message=F}
set.seed(2)
N<-1;P<-0.75;Q=1+6/P;ID<-rep(LETTERS[1:N],each=Q);time<-rep(seq(0,6,by=P),N)
mCl<-9.8;Cl<- mCl*exp(rep(rnorm(sd=0.4,n=N),each=Q))
mV<-12.0;V<-mV*exp(rep(rnorm(sd=0.1,n=N),each=Q))
ke<-Cl/V 
Dose<-40 
onoral<- function(t) (Dose/V)*exp(-(Cl/V)*t)
conc<- signif(onoral(time),2) 
error<-rnorm(mean=0,sd=0.3,n=length(conc))
conc<-conc+conc*error+rnorm(mean=0,sd=0.2,n=length(conc)) # additive and proportional residual error
fru<-data.frame(ID,time,conc)

ggplot(fru,aes(x=time,y=conc))+
  geom_point()+
  theme_bw(base_size=18)+
  theme(legend.position="none")+
  ylab("Plasma concentration (mg/L)")+
  xlab("Time (hr)")
ggplotRegression <- function (fit) {
require(ggplot2)
ggplot(fit$model, aes_string(x = names(fit$model)[2], y = names(fit$model)[1])) + 
  geom_point()+
  theme_bw(base_size=18)+
  theme(legend.position="none")+
  ylab("Log plasma concentration (mg/L)")+
  xlab("Time (hr)") +
  stat_smooth(method = "lm", col = "red",se=F,alpha=0.6)+
  annotate("text", x=4.2, y=1.4, hjust = 0,label=paste("C0 =",signif(fit$coef[[1]],2)))+
  annotate("text", x=4.2, y=1.2, hjust = 0,label=paste( "ke =",-signif(fit$coef[[2]], 2)))+
  annotate("text", x=4.2, y=1, hjust = 0,label=paste( "p =",signif(summary(fit)$coef[2,4], 2)))
  
}
ggplotRegression(lm(log(conc) ~ time, data = fru))
```

# Population pharmacokinetic modelling

## What is mixed effects modelling?

In a population pharmacokinetic model, we assume that each individual's pharmacokinetic parameters (e.g. clearance) are made up of two components.

The fixed effect represents the typical value across the population. For example, the typical clearance of Drug A in healthy volunteers. This is what we usually report as the headline result of a population pharmacokinetic analysis.

The random effect represents how much any given individual deviates from that typical value. Not everyone has the same clearance. Some patients clear the drug faster, some slower. The random effect captures this between-subject variability. We cannot predict it perfectly, but we can estimate how large it is.

A mixed-effects model contains both components. 

### Using a compartmental pharmacokinetic model to provide an example of what we mean

Let's return to the one-compartment IV-bolus model once more. Let us parameterise using *primary* pharmacokinetic parameters (clearance and volume of distribution). Broadly, our model remains the same $$C=C_{0}e^{-\frac{Cl}{V}t}$$ however, we now add a further layer, by stating that our parameters are made up of a fixed (population mean, $\theta$) effect and a random (inter-individual variability, $\eta$) effect. $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_{Cl}}$$ the modelling process then estimates $$\theta_{Cl}\textrm{ -the population mean value for clearance (a fixed effect)}$$ and makes some estimate of $$\eta_{Cl} \textrm{ -individual variability of clearance (a random-effect)}$$

Similarly, we model volume of distribution as $$V=\theta_{V}\times e^{\eta_{V}}$$ 

# Non-linear mixed-effects modelling in <TT>R</TT>  

Non-linear mixed-effects modelling is computationally complex and requires knowledge of statistics, pharmacological models and how to use computer code. We are going to enter into the world of population pharmacokinetic modelling by using the package <TT>nlmixr</TT>. 

## Using <TT>nlmixr</TT>

First you need to call <TT>library(nlmixr2)</TT> to load the package. 

In this example we fit a one-compartment oral-absorption model to sparse data after an oral dose of furosemide is taken. 

```{r fruhead,warning=F,message=F}
sparse<-
 read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dlonsdal/SGUL_PK_data/main/sparsefru.csv")

kbl(head(sparse), booktabs = TRUE, caption = "First few rows of the furosemide dataset") %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("striped"))

```

<TT>nlmixr2</TT> requires data to be structured and labelled in a particular way. 

* ID = patient ID
* TIME = time (since administration of dose)
* DV = dependent variable (drug concentration)
* AMT = amount (dose)
* EVID = event identifier (tells <TT>nlmixr2</TT> whether that row represents an observation or dose event)
* CMT = compartment (1 = depot compartment, 2 = central compartment).

Then you need to build your model. When building the model, you specify two blocks in a standard <TT>R function()</TT>. As always, you give your function a name, I have chosen <TT>one.cmt</TT>. 

### Ini block

The first part of the function is to set the initial estimates for your model parameters, this is known as the <TT>ini()</TT> block. We are parameterising using $k_a$, $Cl$ and $V$ in our one-compartment oral-absorption model. So we need to tell <TT>nlmixr</TT> initial values for each of these parameters *as well as* values for the random effects associated with each parameter. 

### Model block

The second part of the function defines the model. The <TT>model()</TT> block. Here you describe the relationship between the effects. 

After building the model we can run a check using <TT>nlmixr(one.cmt)</TT>, which describes the model we have built. And then we fit the model to the data.

```{r nlmixr,warning=F,message=F}

sparse<-
 read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dlonsdal/SGUL_PK_data/main/sparsefru.csv")


# First make the model
one.cmt <- function() {
  ini({ 
    tka <-log(1.3) # set the initial estimates for each parameter, note that ka will not be required for IV datasets
    tcl <- log(8) 
    tv <- log(20)
    eta.ka~0.1 # inter-individual variabilities
    eta.cl ~ 0.1
    eta.v ~ 0.1
    add.sd <- 0.01 # residual variability
  })
  model({ # define the model
    ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka) # ka will not be required for IV datasets
    cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)
    v <- exp(tv + eta.v)
    linCmt() ~ add(add.sd)
  })
}

# Run a check
nlmixr2(one.cmt)

# Fit the data using this model
fit<-nlmixr2(one.cmt,sparse,est="saem", list(print=0))

```

You can run <TT>print(fit)</TT> and you will get an enormous amount of output from your model. Tables  \@ref(tab:nlmixfix) & \@ref(tab:nlmixran) show the fixed and random parameter estimates, along with confidence intervals. Don't worry about the other output for now.
```{r nlmixfix,echo=F,warning=T,message=F}

kbl(fit$par.fixed %>% 
      select(-last_col(),-6),booktabs = T, caption="Fixed effect parameter estimates from Pop-PK analysis of sparse furosemide data")%>%  kable_styling(latex_options =c("striped"))


```

```{r nlmixran,echo=F,warning=T,message=F}
kbl(signif(diag(fit$omegaR),2), caption="Random effects parameter estimates from Pop-PK analysis of sparse furosemide data",booktabs = T,col.names = c(
                           "Estimate"))%>%  kable_styling(latex_options =c("striped"))
```

# For interest: Non-linear mixed-effects modelling explained in a bit more detail

## Linear versus non-linear models

Linear models are (relatively) easy to solve and calculate estimates for model parameters. Linear transformations are not always possible. When undertaking a non-linear approach, computer programs use an *iterative approach* to finding values for model parameters. With an iterative approach, a series of values for model parameters are tested to see which provide the values for model parameters that best fit the data. 

The biggest difference you will notice in your introduction to non-linear modelling is that you will now be required to input an *initial estimate* for model parameters, i.e. you will have to give <TT>R</TT> a value for clearance and volume of distribution that you want it to start at in its search for its final estimate of the population value of that parameter. You give the program your best guess of what the true population value is and then the program tries to improve on it. 

## Fixed-effects, random-effects and mixed-effects models
Fixed-effects in an experiment or trial are controlled, or the same, for each individual. For example, drug dose and frequency of administration are usually fixed. They may also be variables where all possible values/levels are studied. For example, if you want to study the effect of smoking on the kinetics of a drug, you would study the drug kinetics in smokers- and non-smokers. The 'smoking' effect in your model is a fixed-effect since all levels of this factor (categorical)-variable are studied. 

Random-effects in an experiment or trial are less controlled and do not include all possible levels. For example, we take a sample of participants in a trial, but we really want to know/make inferences about the population from which they are sampled. Since we do not study every single subject from the population, the participants in the study are a random effect. 

Mixed-effects is simply a model which contains fixed- and random-effects. 

## A note on $\eta$

Let's imagine an individual in a clinical trial, lets call them individual $i$, or the $i$th individual. For $i$, their value of clearance will correspond to $$Cl_i=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_i}$$ now, it should be noted that $\eta$ is assumed to follow a normal distribution with mean of zero and standard deviation $\omega_{Cl}$ The mathematical notation for this is as follows $$\eta\sim N(0,\omega_{Cl}^2)$$ you will see from this that in a non-linear mixed-effects model, we do not estimate the population mean value of $\eta$ (since it is always zero), we estimate the variance, $\omega^2$. Again, we have to tell our computer program a value to start looking for this. 

You will finally note, that by choosing to model $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_{Cl}}$$ we are modelling clearance with a log-normal distribution. We *could* choose to model it $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}+ \eta_{Cl}$$but as we have previously demonstrated, our *prior* knowledge of clearance is that it is likely to have a log-normal distribution so we should choose to model it as such. 

## $\mu$ referencing
You will note in the <TT>nlmixr</TT> code that we seem to for no good reason input a '$log(value)$' for our initial parameter estimates and then place them in the exponential domain in our <TT>model()</TT> block of code. This is known as $\mu$ referencing. 

Remember, we are modelling fixed and random effects for our model parameters $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_{Cl}}$$ now, it just so happens that the estimation algorithms work a little better if all of the parameters being estimated are in the exponential domain. We have to write our model with a little mathematical fiddling to achieve this. Recall that $$e^a\times e^b=e^{a+b}\qquad  \textrm{AND}\qquad  e^{log(c)}=c$$ so instead of inputting $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_{Cl}}$$ into our model we instead input $$Cl= e^{log(\theta_{Cl})+\eta_{Cl}}$$ you will have seen in Table \@ref(tab:nlmixfix) that <TT>nlmixr</TT> estimates $log(\theta)$ and then automatically back-transforms the final parameter estimates to the standard domain.

## Residual variability
In our non-linear mixed-effects model, we are more sophisticated about how we model variability, applying $\eta's$ to the model parameters $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_{Cl}}$$ but these random-effects will not explain all of the deviation of the observed data. There will still be some left over, residual, error. So we add a residual error component to our model. 

This is done in the <TT>linCmt() $\sim$ add(add.sd)</TT> line of code. Here we tell <TT>nlmixr</TT> that we wish our residual error to be modelled in an additive fashion, i.e. $$C=\frac{F.D.k_a}{V_d.(k_a-k_e)}.(e^{-k_et}-e^{-k_at})+\epsilon$$ again, we assume this residual error follows a normal distribution with mean $0$ and variance $\sigma^2$ $$\epsilon\sim N(0,\sigma^2)$$ and you will have noticed that we give <TT>nlmixr</TT> a starting value for $\sigma$. 

# Components of a mixed-effects pharmacokinetic model
We have above described two key parts of a pharmacokinetic model. The **structural** model and the **statistical** model. 

## The structural model

The structural model is the underlying equation that describes the mean population trend. In short, this is the compartmental model you have chosen to model the data with. In the example above this was the one-compartment oral-absorption model. $$C=\frac{F.D.k_a}{V_d.(k_a-k_e)}.(e^{-k_et}-e^{-k_at})$$

## The statistical model

The statistical model is how we have chosen to model the parameter- and residual- variability.

Parameter variability you will recall referred to the $\eta$ terms, for example $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_{Cl}}$$ where $$\eta\sim N(0,\omega_{Cl}^2)$$

And our residual error model was the additive $+\epsilon$ where $$\epsilon\sim N(0,\sigma^2)$$

Later in the course we will add a further component - the **covariate model**.
